metadata.title: Quick-fire: When circles overlap
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Companion to qf-ingroup-001 (clean social-distance gradients) and qf-ingroup-003 (different kinds of community). This round probes more layered situations where the user is in multiple groups at once — generational, tradition-vs-newcomer, dual-community, moderator-with-conflicting-loyalties. The same loyalty-vs-universalism axis, but the choice is now often between two in-groups rather than in-group-vs-out-group.
metadata.domain_anchor: Brewer optimal-distinctiveness theory; Roccas & Brewer 2002 on social identity complexity; Cikara/Bruneau parochial-empathy extended to multi-group identification.

items.prompt: A younger family member is breaking from an established family tradition you grew up with. Older family members are quietly upset. The younger person asks for your support.

options.text: Support the younger family member openly. Traditions adapt; your loyalty is to the person, not the inherited form.
Stay neutral or gently encourage them to compromise; the tradition has held the family together for a reason.

items.prompt: A stranger you've never met reaches out — they're an alum of your school / former member of a group you belonged to. They're asking for help that's reasonable but real.

options.text: Help them. The shared affiliation creates a real obligation worth honoring.
Treat them like any other stranger asking the same question. The shared affiliation is thin grounds for special treatment.

items.prompt: You moderate (informally or formally) an online community. A long-time member is being repeatedly hostile to a newer member in ways that fall just short of the explicit rules but clearly violate the spirit.

options.text: Defend the newer member publicly: 'This isn't how we treat people who join here.'
Reach out privately to the long-timer first; they've been part of the community a long time and deserve direct rather than public correction.

items.prompt: Two of your friend groups have an unspoken dislike of each other. A close friend from Group A asks you to bring them to an event you'd normally attend with Group B.

options.text: Bring them. Your friend is your friend; the inter-group tension isn't theirs to navigate.
Suggest going separately; the Group B event isn't a context where you can integrate Group A without friction for everyone.

items.prompt: Someone wants to join a niche community you're part of (a hobby, a profession, a creative scene). They share the interest sincerely but don't yet have any of the markers established members use to recognize each other.

options.text: Welcome them in actively — introduce them, lower the markers' importance, help them find their footing.
Be friendly but let them earn their place the way you and others did. The markers exist for a reason.

items.prompt: Two of your peer groups (e.g., work friends and college friends) are going to overlap at a shared event. One group makes inside jokes about something the other group will visibly not get.

options.text: Quietly defuse — change the subject if the joke starts, make space for the other group.
Let it play out. Both groups can handle a little awkwardness; you don't need to be the social mediator.
